posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I love the Francois Bertheau wines but most of my experience is with the Chambolle Village and Chambolle 1er Cru.

I noticed that the vineyards from which the 1er is made are clustered below Bonnes Mares (my understanding is that it comes from Gruenchers, Noirots, Groseilles, and Baudes). The other single vineyard wines are from Charmes and Amoureuses on the other side of the village.

My question for people who have experience with the single vineyard wines is: Does the 1er Cru blend show you any particular terroir signature, and if so is it more Bonnes Mares/Morey-like than the other wines (I’m not certain if that is generally true of these vineyards)? And how does it compare to the three single vineyard wines?

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Craig G:

I love the Francois Bertheau wines but most of my experience is with the Chambolle Village and Chambolle 1er Cru.

I noticed that the vineyards from which the 1er is made are clustered below Bonnes Mares (my understanding is that it comes from Gruenchers, Noirots, Groseilles, and Baudes). The other single vineyard wines are from Charmes and Amoureuses on the other side of the village.

My question for people who have experience with the single vineyard wines is: Does the 1er Cru blend show you any particular terroir signature, and if so is it more Bonnes Mares/Morey-like than the other wines (I’m not certain if that is generally true of these vineyards)? And how does it compare to the three single vineyard wines?

My experience with Bertheau is limited, but the four 1er Crus above seem to be correct.

I´ve attended a Chambolle-Musigny 1er Crus tasting last fall, and generally I would say that the most resemblance to Bonnes-Mares showes Les Fuées … and then maybe Gruenchers (Dujac) … Baudes and Noirots lacking a bit of fullness and intensity…

Also generally the Northern 1er Crus are slightly darker and show harder tannins, often lacking sweetness …
the Southern Crus like Amoureuses, but also Charmes are brighter coloured, are sweeter and more elegant, but seem to be often lighter on the palate …

From my memory (never had Berteaus different Crus side by side) I´d say that the 1er Cru definitely shows slightly darker fruit, but his style is generally not in deep colour, so it´s more a shade …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.cooked: I’ve tasted the complete several times side by side and I like the premier cru in general a bit more than the Charmes because it has a bit more shoulders. Yes it is less ethereal and darker than the charmes or amoureuses but I would also not call it a “light version” of the bonnes mares. The premier cru is vinified in 2 “botti” (I don’t know the correct word, large barrels), he has one old and one that was exchanged in 2015 so the 2015 version has a bit more new oak than regular (but the effect from the botti is limited off course)

posts.name: D@vid R0sewater
posts.cooked: I concur with the consensus here, slightly darker fruit, but not really Bonnes Mares-like. I think the 1er is the QPR winner as between the village, 1er and Charmes, fwiw.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I’d say that above all its tastes like a Bertheau wine - perfumed, supple, delicate. Since even their Bonnes Mares is quite ethereal, saying it nods in that direction isn’t saying that much.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
PCLIN:
D@vid R0sewater:

I think the 1er is the QPR winner as between the village, 1er and Charmes, fwiw.

Agree with you there, I prefer the 1er Cru to the Charmes, and Amoureuses over Bonnes Mares partly because I am too old to wait for Bonnes Mares to come around.

I prefer the Amoureuses to any other wines from Francois but that is just a personal preference. After that it is Bonnes Mares and Charmes. I do like the 1er quite a bit. It is more like Fuees or Baudes in character. Rocky in a way. Not that deep or complex like Bonnes Mares but darker fruit with amazing texture and penetrating fruit. I enjoy it quite a bit. All of the wines are great. FWIW.
